steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Typhlitis (neutropenic enterocolitis) given immunosuppressed state; intra-abdominal abscess or infectious colitis; mesenteric ischemia; gynecologic pathology; bowel obstruction
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the RLQ pain in an immunocompromised patient cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone — no imaging has been performed yet to characterize the intra-abdominal structures.
ensemble.expected_finding: Periappendiceal inflammation/fat stranding consistent with appendicitis, or colonic wall thickening (typhlitis/infectious colitis), or pericolic fat stranding suggesting diverticulitis, or an abscess collection in the RLQ
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: RLQ pain with fever, tachycardia, and leukocytosis in an immunosuppressed patient (aplastic anemia on cyclosporine, prior ATG, steroids) with no prior abdominal imaging warrants CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast as the first-line cross-sectional study to identify the anatomic source and guide urgent management.
grounding: History: 'P/w RLQ pain with no radiation and subjective fevers that's been worsening since ___ with gradual onset'
History: 'pain became intolerable, no PO intake'
Physical exam: 'moderate RLQ abdominal tenderness, - rebound tenderness. bowel sounds present throughout. abdomen soft'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 14.3 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)', 'Neutrophils: 87.7%', 'Absolute Neutrophil Count: 12.53 K/uL'
Imaging already resulted: '(none — only the baseline workup below was available)'
ensemble.reasoning: This immunocompromised patient (aplastic anemia on cyclosporine with subtherapeutic levels, prior ATG/steroids) presents with progressive RLQ pain, fever, and tachycardia with leukocytosis, but no prior abdominal imaging exists. The differential is broad — appendicitis, diverticulitis (known diverticulosis), typhlitis, or abscess — and the physical exam alone cannot distinguish these in an obese, immunosuppressed patient who may have blunted peritoneal signs. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the most efficient single test to anatomically localize the source, characterize the extent of inflammation or infection, and guide whether surgical or medical management is needed.

The differential is broad — appendicitis, diverticulitis (known diverticulosis), typhlitis, or abscess — and the physical exam alone cannot distinguish these in an obese, immunosuppressed patient who may have blunted peritoneal signs. vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated mesenteric fat stranding adjacent to the cecum centered at a small inflamed diverticulum, with a small focus of air equivocal for microperforation, while the appendix, cecal wall, and terminal ileum appeared normal.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The finding of pericolic/periappendiceal fat stranding with an inflamed cecal diverticulum matches the expected category of pericolic fat stranding/diverticulitis in the RLQ, directly resolving the anatomic source of pain.
